comment: Lesson content by stage. Step types: text {heading?, body}, tip {body}, chord {chordId, body?}, practice {heading?, body?, checklist[]}, metronome {bpm, body}. Stages 3-4 arrive in Phase 2, stage 5 in Phase 3.

stages.title: First Steps
stages.subtitle: Get comfortable and make your very first sounds.

lessons.title: Meet your guitar
lessons.summary: Get comfy and learn the six strings.

steps.heading: Get comfy
steps.body: Sit up in a chair with both feet on the floor. Rest the curve of the guitar on your right leg and let it hug your body. There's no wrong way to feel relaxed.

steps.heading: The six strings
steps.body: Your guitar has six strings. The thickest one is at the top and the thinnest at the bottom. We count them from thickest (6th) to thinnest (1st).
Drop your shoulders and unclench your jaw. Loose and relaxed always sounds better than tense.

steps.heading: Try it
steps.body: Spend a minute just holding your guitar and getting comfortable.
checklist: Rest the guitar on your leg so it doesn't slide
Let your left hand gently cradle the neck
Keep your strumming arm loose and relaxed

lessons.title: Your first sounds
lessons.summary: Strum across the strings and pluck them one by one.

steps.heading: Give it a strum
steps.body: Brush your thumb (or a pick) gently down across all the strings, from thickest to thinnest. Hear that? You're playing guitar already.

steps.heading: One string at a time
steps.body: Now pluck each string on its own, slowly, from the thickest to the thinnest. Listen to how each one sounds a little higher than the last.
Aim for a smooth, even brush when you strum — not too hard. You're stroking the strings, not hitting them.
Get used to the feel of the strings.
checklist: Pluck each string one by one
Notice which strings sound low and which sound high

lessons.title: Your first chord: E minor
lessons.summary: Learn Em — the friendliest first chord.

steps.heading: What's a chord?
steps.body: A chord is a few notes played together to make one full sound. E minor (Em) is the perfect first one — it uses just two fingers.
Place your fingers on the two dots shown. Both go on the second fret, on the two strings next to the thickest one.
Press just behind the metal fret line, not on top of it, and press firmly. Curl your fingers so they don't touch the other strings.

steps.heading: Strum it
steps.body: With your fingers in place, strum all six strings slowly. It should ring out clearly.
checklist: Both fingers pressing on the second fret
Every string rings clearly — no buzzing

steps.body: Now let's add a steady beat. Start the metronome and strum Em once on every click.

stages.title: Making Music
stages.subtitle: Add a second chord and change between them in time.

steps.heading: A new shape
steps.body: A minor (Am) uses three fingers, but it sits right next to the Em shape you already know. You've got this.
Place your three fingers on the dots shown. Don't strum the thickest string — start your strum from the second-thickest.
Keep your fingertips curled and press with the very tips, so each string can ring out freely.
Get the Am shape feeling natural.
checklist: Strum from the 5th string down (skip the thickest)

lessons.title: Your first chord change
lessons.summary: Switch smoothly between Em and Am.

steps.heading: Switching chords
steps.body: The magic of guitar is changing between chords. Em and Am are close cousins, so this is a great first switch to learn.
Start on Em.
Then move to Am. Notice your fingers only shift a little.
Go slowly. It's completely normal for changes to feel clumsy at first — every guitarist started exactly here.
Set a slow beat. Strum Em for four clicks, then Am for four clicks, and keep looping.
Loop the change until it feels smoother.
checklist: Four strums of Em, four strums of Am
Keep the beat steady — don't rush the change
Let it sound a little messy at first; it gets easier fast

lessons.title: Strum in time
lessons.summary: Play steady downstrokes with the beat.

steps.heading: Downstrokes
steps.body: A strumming pattern is just a rhythm. We'll start with the simplest: one downward strum on every beat.
Start the beat and strum Em down once per click. Aim to land right on each click.
Count out loud: 1, 2, 3, 4. Counting really does make your timing steadier.

steps.heading: Put it together
steps.body: Combine everything you've learned into a tiny piece of music.
checklist: Strum Em in time for four beats
Change to Am and strum four beats
Loop it — that's a real progression you're playing!
